content.full_text:
Law Offices of

Hugh Breckenridge

February 27, 1990

Ms. Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

RE: Hendrickson vs. Agran, et al.
Orange County Superior Court Case No. 603194
Request for Informal FPPC Opinion

Dear Ms. Donovan:

This letter is a follow-up to a telephone conversation with
Scott Hallabrin of your office on February 22, 1990, and to request an informal opinion from the Fair Political Practices Commission concerning two issues.

The first issue is whether the attorneys fees paid to defend
Mr. Cameron Cosgrove in the Quo Warranto action to challenge his
entitlement to office constitute expenditures or contributions
for political purposes under the Political Reform Act.

The second issue is whether a determination by the City of
Irvine to pay those fees under Government Code Section 995, which
concerns the duty to defend an act or omission within the scope
of employment, exempts the expenditures from being for political
purposes under the Political Reform Act.

Factual Background

We have previously sent your office pleadings in this case.
Mr. Ray Hendrickson, a taxpayer in the City of Irvine, sought to
enjoin the payment of or to collect as civil penalties the attorneys fees being paid by the City of Irvine to defend the Quo
Warranto action against Mr. Cosgrove.

Mr. Cosgrove received the third highest number of votes in
the Irvine City Council's elections of June 1988 in an election
for two vacant council seats. However, Larry Agran, a sitting
councilmember with two years to go in his term, ran for and was
elected to the newly created post of Mayor, thus leaving his
councilmember's seat vacant.

4199 Campus Drive, Suite 700 • Irvine, CA 92715 • (714) 854-2520

Law Offices of

Hugh Breckenridge

Ms. Kathryn E. Donovan, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
February 27, 1990
Page Two


Under the provisions of the law existing prior to the June
1988 election, the candidate with the next highest number of
votes was to fill the unexpired term of a councilmember elected
Mayor. In June of 1988, the Irvine voters also passed a measure
(Measure D) which by its own terms was to apply to the June 1988
election. Measure D allowed voters to petition for a special
election for a seat vacated. In compliance with an ordinance
drafted by the City Attorney in anticipation of Measure D's passage, more than 7% of the voters petitioned for a special election. The signatures on the petitions were certified by the City
Clerk on July 5, 1988. The term of the Mayor and new councilmembers commenced on July 20, 1988. Measure D was certified and
chartered by the Secretary of State on July 15, 1988.

On July 13, 1988, Mr. Agran resigned from his seat as a city
councilmember. On July 20, 1988, Mr. Agran commenced his term as
Mayor of the City of Irvine. On July 20, 1988, Mrs. Miller and
Mrs. Werner were sworn in to the two elected seats and Mr.
Cosgrove assumed the office vacated by Mr. Agran.

The City Council refused to honor the petition to hold a
special election, and the Quo Warranto action followed after
being certified by the Attorney General. The sole issue in the
Quo Warranto action was whether Mr. Cosgrove was entitled to hold
the office vacated by Mr. Agran.

The Judge in the Quo Warranto action, in his Memorandum
of Tentative Decision, has ruled that Mr. Cosgrove's candidates
oath was a sufficient oath of office and therefore he was entitled to the office and duly elected to it on July 13, 1988,
before Measure D was chaptered and became effective.

Enclosed are copies of two letters written to me by the
Michael D. Rubin for the City of Irvine. The first is dated
January 12, 1990, and encloses a copy of the verbatim excerpts of
Minutes of Special Meeting held in the Irvine City Council on
August 23, 1988, in which the City decided to defend Mr. Cosgrove
in the Quo Warranto action. The second is dated February 16,
1990 and encloses a Memorandum of Tentative Decision in the Quo
Warranto action. I have discussed both of these letters with my
client. As a representative of the public and the taxpayers he
wishes some guidance from the public agency which enforces the
Political Reform Act about the merits of his case.

Law Offices of

Hugh Breckenridge

Ms. Kathryn E. Donovan, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
February 27, 1990
Page Three


     As a result of my conversations with Mr. Hallabrin I under-
stand that an informal opinion can be rendered within 21 days,
but in no event later than 40 days.  My client will be guided by
the Commission's opinion on these two issues in his prosecution
of the case.

     If I can provide any further information or documents con-
cerning the issues, please let me know.  Also note that I am
sending a copy of this letter to Michael D. Rubin at Rutan &
Tucker so that he may provide you with whatever information he
deems relevant to your decision.

     Thank you in advance for your courtesy and cooperation.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        LAW OFFICES OF HUGH BRECKENRIDGE

                                        [signature]

                                        Hugh Breckenridge

HB/lw
cc:  Ray Hendrickson wo/enc.
     Michael D. Rubin, Esq. wo/enc.

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 6, 1990


Hugh Breckenridge
4199 Campus Drive, Suite 700
Irvine, CA 92715

Re: Letter No. 90-175

Dear Mr. Breckenridge:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on March 2, 1990 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If you have any questions about your advice request,
you may contact Scott Hallabrin an attorney in the Legal Division,
directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to
information needed. If your request is for informal assistance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can. (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

KED:plh


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

90-175

Greg: Below is a letter I have written at Kathy's request & background materials that prompted the letter. It involves an Orange Co lawsuit alleging violation of the PRA. the Plaintiffs atty wants to know if we want to participate. Kathy thinks his theory is weak. Apparently, so did the court, which denied his prelim. injunction request. Could you look this over before the letter goes & see if you agree?

Scott
1/2/90

Scott - (or have Jee write letter)
Pls write a brief letter telling him we are not interested in pursuing this case. I've told him on the phone I think his legal theory is off base. We don't want to touch this one.
Thx, Jeed

1/2/90

Scott - I agree with you and Kathy ... "deal" is generous.

[signature - Jog]

Law Offices of

Hugh Breckenridge

December 12, 1989

California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J. Street
Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Attention: Ms. Kathryn Donovan
General Counsel

RE: Hendrickson vs. Agran;
Orange County Superior Court Case Number 603194

Dear Ms. Donovan:

You may recall that the above-referenced case was brought under the Political Reform Act to enjoin, and also seek Civil Penalties, under Government Code Section 91005. The suit challenges the payment of attorneys fees by the City of Irvine to defend Councilmember Cameron Cosgrove in a Quo Warranto action. He was seated as a Councilmember following the June 1988 Municipal Election.

On September 21, 1989 my office provided Notice to you of the commencement of the Action as required by Section 91007 of the Government Code. On December 4, 1989, our application for a Preliminary Injunction was finally heard by the Orange County Superior Court in Department 21 before the Honorable Eileen Moore. The application was denied on the basis that we made an insufficient showing of irreparable injury, and on the basis that the Court was not satisfied that we would succeed on the merits of the case. The defendants are the City, the three Councilmembers who voted to pay the fees, and Councilmember Cosgrove, who has received more than $40,000 in legal fees to defend the case.

The Court denied defendants' request for a bond under Government Code, Section 91012 because the City is providing a defense for them under Government Code, Section 995. The City's defense of this action is also based entirely on its alleged duty to defend Councilmember Cosgrove under Government Code, Section 995.

4199 Campus Drive, Suite 700 • Irvine, CA 92715 • (714) 854-2520

Law Offices of

Hugh Breckenridge

Ms. Kathryn Donovan, General Counsel
California Fair Political Practices Commission
December 12, 1989
Page Two


     The Fair Political Practices Commission has assignment in-
terest in this case.  The attorneys for the individual Council
Member Defendants as well as the City of Irvine have offered to
dismiss the case and to waive their claim for fees.  I there-
fore, am requesting that the Commission provide to me, as attor-
ney for plaintiff, a statement of its interest in the case.  I
would like to know whether it wishes the action to proceed and
whether it wishes to intervene on behalf of the Plaintiff in the
action.

     It appears that the issues may be resolved by Summary Ad-
judication and as a consequence, there is little further work in
terms of trial preparation to be done on the case.  However, the
legal issues are significant regarding your agency's authority
under the Political Reform Act as revised by Proposition 73.
Since my office does not stand to benefit from the civil
Penalties in the event that we prevail in the action, I would ap-
preciate your calling me to discuss the issues.  I will be avail-
able to discuss the issues until December 20, 1989 and then will
not be back in the office until the January 3, 1990.

                              Very truly yours,

                              LAW OFFICES OF HUGH BRECKENRIDGE

                              [signature]

                              Hugh Breckenridge

L. SKI HARRISON

SCOTT M. SCHOENBERG
THOMAS E. TALBOT
SARAH J. KNECHT
DAN SLATER
WILLIAM E. ENFIELD
JAMES S. WEITZ
DANIEL L. BRANDSTINE
PAUL J. HASTINGS
DAVID T. WARD
DARWIN KINGHORN
GREGORY B. TAYLOR
JENNIFER L. GIBSON
ROBERT L. DIEDMAN
PATRICK D. MCCALLA
GREGORY S. JERIELL
PATRICK A. MUNDO

ATTORNEYS AT LAW
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS

CENTRAL BANK TOWER, SUITE 1400
SOUTH COAST PLAZA TOWN CENTER
611 ANTON BOULEVARD
POST OFFICE BOX 950
COSTA MESA, CALIFORNIA 92628-9990

A. W. RUTAN (1896-1972)
JAMES B. TUCKER, SR. (1888-1980)
WILFORD W. DAHL, SR. (1911-1987)
THEODORE N. WALLACE (1914-1974)
DAVID J. GARBALD, III
OF COUNSEL

TELEPHONE (714) 641-8100
(714) 645-7298

TELECOPIER (714) 546-9035

TELEX 910 596/883
CABLE ADDRESS: RUTAN TUC CSMA

March 15, 1990

IN REPLY PLEASE REFER TO

Fair Political Practices Commission
General Counsel's Office
Attention: Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Re: Hendrickson v. Agran, et al.
Orange County Superior Court Case No. 60-31-54
Request by Hugh Breckenridge for Informal Opinion

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     I am writing to follow up on the February 27, 1990
letter sent by Mr. Breckenridge as to whether the City of
Irvine's payment of Councilmember Cosgrove's quo warranto
defense costs constitutes reportable and/or illegal campaign
contributions to Mr. Cosgrove. Mr. Breckenridge represents
the individual plaintiff in the above-referenced litigation
and this office represents the City of Irvine and the
individual city council members named as defendants.

     Mr. Breckenridge's February 27, 1990 letter states that
certain of the court papers were previously sent to your
office. Not knowing what specific documents Mr. Breckenridge
mailed to you, I have enclosed a copy of our Memorandum of
Points and Authorities (dated October 31, 1989) in opposition
to Mr. Breckenridge's motion for a preliminary injunction.
The motion sought to enjoin the City of Irvine's payment of

*A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

RUTAN & TUCKER
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS


Fair Political Practices Commission
Attention: Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
March 15, 1990
Page 2


Councilmember Cosgrove's defense costs incurred in connection
with a quo warranto action.

     As demonstrated in the opposition papers (at pages 7-
14), the City was not only authorized to but had a legal duty
pursuant to Government Code Section 995 to provide
Councilmember Cosgrove with a defense. A Councilmember is
entitled to the benefits of a defense under Government Code
Section 995 as an incident of his or her office or employment
just like he or she is entitled to a salary. This is true
even for a defense to office holders who later are found to
be improperly in office. (See, for example, Chubbuck v.
Wilson (1907) 151 Cal. 162, 168.)

     As evidenced by the ruling in the quo warranto action
(which was enclosed in Mr. Breckenridge's letter), the court
ultimately determined that Mr. Cosgrove was properly in
office. Because Mr. Cosgrove provided consideration (serving
as a Councilmember) for the payment of his defense costs and
because the payment was a statutory obligation, the
voluntariness necessary for a campaign contribution is
absent. It is no more a campaign contribution than an
insurance company's obligatory payment of defense costs under
an insurance policy would be.

     In ruling on Mr. Breckenridge's motion for a preliminary
injunction, the court addressed the merits of his case and
found that (among other things) he had not shown a
probability of prevailing on the merits of his Political
Reform Act allegations. (See enclosed December 4, 1989
Reporter's Transcript at page 31, lines 6-7.) It may be
notable to point out that before it authorized payment of
Councilmember Cosgrove's defense costs, the City Council was
advised by its City Attorney that Government Code Section 995
required the City to pay such costs.

     We can provide you with a detailed legal analysis if you
deem it helpful and appropriate. We also disagree with the
factual representations set forth in Mr. Breckenridge's
letter. Since we are not intimately familiar with the FPPC's

RUTAN & TUCKER
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS


Fair Political Practices Commission
Attention: Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
March 15, 1990
Page 3


procedures in these types of matters, we would ask that you
inform us if you feel a more extensive legal analysis is
desired.

Very truly yours,

RUTAN & TUCKER

Philip D. Kohn

PDK/jb

sections.question: The first issue is whether the attorneys fees paid to defend Mr. Cameron Cosgrove in the Quo warranto action to challenge his entitlement to office constitute expenditures or contributions for political purposes under the Political Reform Act. The second issue is whether a determination by the City of Irvine to pay those fees under Government Code Section 995, which concerns the duty to defend an act or omission within the scope of employment, exempts the expenditures from being for political purposes under the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether attorney's fees paid by the City of Irvine to defend councilmember Cameron Cosgrove in a quo warranto action constitute political expenditures under the Political Reform Act, and whether the city's duty to defend under Government Code Section 995 exempts such fees from being characterized as political expenditures.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple letters: (1) Feb 27, 1990 incoming request from Hugh Breckenridge requesting FPPC informal advice with detailed factual background; (2) Mar 6, 1990 FPPC acknowledgment letter confirming receipt; (3) Dec 12, 1989 letter (misdated or earlier correspondence) requesting FPPC statement of interest in the case; (4) Partial law firm letterhead from Rutan & Tucker at end. The main substantive request is the Feb 27, 1990 letter. Marked as 'incoming_request' because this is the lawyer's request to FPPC, not FPPC's advice response.

The second issue is whether a determination by the City of Irvine to pay those fees under Government Code Section 995, which concerns the duty to defend an act or omission within the scope of employment, exempts the expenditures from being for political purposes under the Political Reform Act.

embedding.summary: This document is a request for informal FPPC advice regarding whether attorney's fees paid by the City of Irvine to defend councilmember Cameron Cosgrove in a quo warranto action constitute political expenditures under the Political Reform Act. The document includes factual background about the 1988 Irvine city council election, Cosgrove's appointment to office, and the resulting legal challenge to his entitlement to the seat.
